content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6c49646a

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable personal travel history and trip-planning preferences plus broader reusable context from recommendations and decisions, and force_memory_write requires a non-noop route.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I'm / I / we / our; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements need the stable USER entity as subject; no existing candidates are provided.
- mention=Denver; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=LOCATION; name=Denver, Colorado; reason=Concrete origin location in the road trip discussion; no existing candidate.
- mention=Mount Rushmore; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=LOCATION; name=Mount Rushmore; reason=Concrete destination/location discussed repeatedly; no existing candidate.
- mention=Mount Rushmore National Memorial; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=LOCATION; name=Mount Rushmore National Memorial; reason=Specific memorial destination and shuttle endpoint; distinct enough from the mountain/site mention for later claims.
- mention=Yellowstone National Park; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=LOCATION; name=Yellowstone National Park; reason=Concrete prior trip destination; no existing candidate.
- mention=Jackson, Wyoming; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=LOCATION; name=Jackson, Wyoming; reason=Concrete first-day destination in the remembered Yellowstone trip; no existing candidate.
- mention=Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=LOCATION; name=Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA; reason=Specific campground/accommodation option the user selected; no existing candidate.
- mention=deluxe cabin rental; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PRODUCT; name=Deluxe cabin rental at Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA; reason=Concrete selected accommodation product/service that later claims may reference.
- mention=shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=SERVICE; name=Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA shuttle service; reason=Specific campground shuttle service discussed with reusable details like cost and frequency.
- mention=Keystone; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=LOCATION; name=Keystone, South Dakota; reason=Concrete nearby town for restaurants and campgrounds; no existing candidate.
- mention=Hill City; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=LOCATION; name=Hill City, South Dakota; reason=Concrete nearby town for restaurant recommendations; no existing candidate.
- mention=Rapid City; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=LOCATION; name=Rapid City, South Dakota; reason=Concrete nearby city for accommodations and restaurants; no existing candidate.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Yellowstone National Park|Mount Rushmore|Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA|Denver|Jackson Wyoming
        text_queries=Yellowstone trip last month 4-day 1200 miles family first day 300 miles Jackson Wyoming|Mount Rushmore trip planning deluxe cabin rental KOA shuttle camping preferences|Denver to Mount Rushmore road trip camping instead of hotel
        predicate_hints=attended_event|metric_observation|prefers|has_goal

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:796d6bb9fbd4397b8497051232200fcd] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:6c49646a:session:1; title=LongMemEval 6c49646a session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/20 (Sat) 05:58
Transcript:
user: I'm planning another road trip and I'd like to know the best route from Denver to Mount Rushmore. By the way, speaking of road trips, I just got back from an amazing 4-day trip to Yellowstone National Park with my family last month, where we covered a total of 1,200 miles.
assistant: That sounds like an amazing trip to Yellowstone! 1,200 miles in 4 days is quite an adventure! I'm sure you must have seen some incredible sights.

Now, let's plan your next road trip to Mount Rushmore! The most popular and scenic route from Denver to Mount Rushmore is approximately 570 miles and takes around 8-9 hours of driving time, depending on traffic and the number of stops you make along the way. Here's a suggested route:

**Day 1: Denver, CO to Mount Rushmore, SD**

1. From Denver, take I-25 N towards Cheyenne, WY (approximately 100 miles, 1.5 hours)
2. Continue on I-25 N to I-90 E towards Rapid City, SD (approximately 270 miles, 4 hours)
3. Take exit 57 for US-16 E towards Mount Rushmore National Memorial (approximately 25 miles, 30 minutes)

**Scenic Stops along the way:**

* Cheyenne, WY: Stop by the Wyoming State Capitol building, the Cheyenne Frontier Days Old West Museum, or the Terry Bison Ranch.
* Wall, SD: Visit the famous Wall Drug Store, a popular roadside attraction.
* Badlands National Park, SD: Take a short detour to explore the stunning Badlands Loop Scenic Byway (SD-240) and enjoy the unique geological formations.

**Tips and Recommendations:**

* Be sure to check the Mount Rushmore National Memorial website for any road closures or construction before your trip.
* Consider visiting the Crazy Horse Memorial, a massive mountain sculpture located just 17 miles from Mount Rushmore.
* Book your accommodations in Rapid City or Keystone, SD, as they offer a wide range of hotels, restaurants, and amenities.
* Don't forget to try some local cuisine, like chislic (a South Dakota specialty) or a classic American burger at a roadside diner.

Enjoy your road trip to Mount Rushmore!
user: That sounds like a great route. Can you recommend any good camping sites near Mount Rushmore? We're considering camping instead of staying in a hotel.
assistant: Camping near Mount Rushmore is a fantastic way to experience the great outdoors and be close to the monument. Here are some excellent camping options to consider:

**National Park Service Campgrounds:**

1. **Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA**: This Kampgrounds of America (KOA) location is within walking distance to the monument and offers tent sites, RV sites, and cabins. Amenities include a pool, playground, and convenience store.
2. **Custer State Park**: Located about 20 miles from Mount Rushmore, this state park offers several campgrounds with varying amenities. The Sylvan Lake Campground is a popular option, with electric hookups and modern restrooms.

**Private Campgrounds:**

1. **Rushmore RV Resort**: This resort offers full-hookup RV sites, tent sites, and cabins. Amenities include a pool, hot tub, and convenience store.
2. **American Presidents Resort**: This resort features RV sites, tent sites, and cabins, as well as an outdoor pool, playground, and BBQ area.
3. **Keystone RV Park**: Located in the heart of Keystone, this park offers full-hookup RV sites, tent sites, and cabins. Amenities include a pool, hot tub, and convenience store.

**National Forest Campgrounds:**

1. **Deer Mountain Campground**: Located in the Black Hills National Forest, about 10 miles from Mount Rushmore, this campground offers dispersed camping with basic amenities.
2. **Horsethief Lake Campground**: Another option in the Black Hills National Forest, about 15 miles from Mount Rushmore, with basic amenities and scenic views.

**Tips and Reservations:**

* Make reservations well in advance, especially during peak season (June to August).
* Check the campground's website or contact them directly for availability and to book your spot.
* Be prepared for variable weather conditions, including thunderstorms and cooler evenings, even during the summer.
* Bring insect repellent, as mosquitoes and other bugs can be prevalent in the Black Hills.

Remember to follow the rules and regulations of the campground you choose, and enjoy your camping experience near Mount Rushmore!
user: I'm interested in the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA. Can you tell me more about the amenities and activities they offer? Also, do they have any RV rentals available?
assistant: The Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA is a fantastic choice for camping near the monument. Here's what you can expect:

**Amenities:**

1. **Camp Sites:** The KOA offers a range of camp sites, including:
	* Tent sites with electric hookups
	* RV sites with full hookups (water, electric, and sewer)
	* Deluxe RV sites with patios and grills
	* Cabin rentals (see below)
2. **Amenities:**
	* **Pool:** A large outdoor pool with a water slide and separate kiddie pool
	* **Playground:** A playground area for kids with climbing structures and swings
	* **Convenience Store:** A well-stocked store with groceries, souvenirs, and RV supplies
	* **Laundry:** Coin-operated laundry facilities
	* **Restrooms:** Clean, modern restrooms with showers and flush toilets
	* **Wi-Fi:** Free Wi-Fi throughout the campground
	* **Dog Park:** A fenced dog park for your furry friends to exercise
3. **Recreational Activities:**
	* **Mini Golf:** A 18-hole mini golf course on site
	* **Game Room:** An indoor game room with arcade games and pool tables
	* **Hiking Trails:** Access to nearby hiking trails, including the Presidential Trail that takes you to the base of Mount Rushmore
	* **Shuttle Service:** A shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial (seasonal)

**Cabin Rentals:**

The KOA offers a range of cabin rentals, including:

1. **Deluxe Cabins:** These cabins feature a full kitchen, private bathroom, and sleeping accommodations for up to 6 people.
2. **Kamping Kabin:** A more rustic option with a kitchenette and sleeping accommodations for up to 4 people.
3. **Tent Cabin:** A tent-style cabin with sleeping accommodations for up to 4 people.

**RV Rentals:**

Yes, the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA offers RV rentals! They have a fleet of RVs available for rent, ranging from small travel trailers to larger motorhomes. The RV rentals come with amenities like kitchenettes, bathrooms, and sleeping accommodations.

**RV Rental Options:**

1. **Travel Trailers:** Sleep 2-4 people, with kitchenette and bathroom
2. **Motorhomes:** Sleep 4-6 people, with full kitchen and bathroom
3. **Pop-Up Campers:** Sleep 2-4 people, with kitchenette and bathroom

**Reservations:**

To book a campsite, cabin, or RV rental, visit the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA website or call them directly at (800) 562-1885. Be sure to make reservations well in advance, especially during peak season.

Remember to check the KOA's website for any updates on amenities, activities, and RV rentals, as they may change seasonally.
user: I think we'll go with the deluxe cabin rental. Can you tell me more about the shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial? Is it free, and how often does it run?
assistant: The deluxe cabin rental is a great choice.

**Shuttle Service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial:**

The Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA offers a convenient shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial, making it easy to visit the monument without worrying about parking or traffic. Here are the details:

**Shuttle Service Details:**

1. **Seasonal Operation:** The shuttle service typically runs from late May to early October, coinciding with the peak tourist season.
2. **Frequency:** The shuttle runs every 30 minutes, starting from 8:00 AM to 6:00 PM. The last shuttle departs the KOA at 5:30 PM.
3. **Duration:** The shuttle ride takes approximately 10-15 minutes each way.
4. **Pickup/Drop-off:** The shuttle picks up and drops off at the KOA's main office. From there, you'll be taken to the Mount Rushmore National Memorial parking lot.
5. **Cost:** The shuttle service is **free** for KOA guests! You don't need to pay anything extra to use this convenient service.

**Tips:**

1. **Plan Ahead:** Be sure to check the shuttle schedule in advance to plan your day accordingly.
2. **Arrive Early:** To avoid long lines, consider taking an early shuttle to Mount Rushmore National Memorial.
3. **Bring Water and Snacks:** Bring water, snacks, and sunscreen to ensure a comfortable trip.
4. **Be Prepared for Security:** Be prepared for security checks at the memorial, and plan accordingly.

By using the shuttle service, you'll save time, avoid parking hassles, and enjoy a convenient trip to Mount Rushmore National Memorial. Just remember to check the KOA's website or contact them directly for any updates on the shuttle service schedule.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any more questions.
user: I'm glad I could get some helpful information about the shuttle service. By the way, I was thinking about our Yellowstone trip last month, and I realized that we drove around 300 miles on the first day to reach Jackson, Wyoming. That was a long drive, but the scenic views were totally worth it.
assistant: I'm glad you have fond memories of your Yellowstone trip! 300 miles on the first day is indeed a long drive, but as you said, the scenic views must have been breathtaking. Jackson, Wyoming, is a beautiful town, and I'm sure it was a great starting point for your Yellowstone adventure.

The drive from Denver to Jackson is quite scenic, with the Rocky Mountains as a stunning backdrop. You must have passed through some picturesque towns and landscapes, like the Snowy Range and the Wind River Canyon. The Grand Teton National Park, just north of Jackson, is also a treat, with its towering peaks and pristine lakes.

It's great that you prioritized enjoying the views over the long drive. Sometimes, the journey is just as important as the destination, and it sounds like you made the most of your time on the road.

Now, I'm excited for your upcoming trip to Mount Rushmore! With the KOA's shuttle service and the deluxe cabin rental, you'll be all set for a comfortable and convenient trip. Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further recommendations.
user: I'm thinking about the food options near Mount Rushmore. Are there any good restaurants or cafes in the area that you would recommend?
assistant: Mount Rushmore is surrounded by the beautiful Black Hills, and you'll find a variety of delicious food options in the nearby towns of Keystone, Hill City, and Rapid City. Here are some recommendations:

**Keystone:**

1. **The Keystone Café**: A local favorite serving breakfast, lunch, and dinner. Try their famous buffalo burgers or homemade pie.
2. **The Ruby House Restaurant**: Offers upscale American cuisine with a focus on local ingredients. Enjoy their patio seating with mountain views.
3. **The Alpine Inn**: This German-inspired restaurant serves traditional dishes like schnitzel and sauerbraten, along with American favorites.

**Hill City:**

1. **The Hill City Café**: A cozy café serving breakfast, lunch, and baked goods. Try their cinnamon rolls or homemade soup.
2. **The Alpine Slide Restaurant**: Located near the Hill City Alpine Slide, this restaurant serves burgers, sandwiches, and salads, with a focus on locally sourced ingredients.
3. **The 1880 Train's Hill City Depot Café**: This café is located in the historic train depot and offers sandwiches, salads, and snacks, along with a souvenir shop.

**Rapid City:**

1. **The Independent Ale House**: A craft brewery and restaurant serving burgers, sandwiches, and salads, with a focus on local ingredients.
2. **The Firehouse Brewing Company**: Another local brewery with a restaurant serving American cuisine, including burgers, steaks, and pasta dishes.
3. **Delmonico Grill**: An upscale restaurant serving steak, seafood, and pasta dishes in a historic building.

**Other Recommendations:**

1. **Mount Rushmore's Carver's Café**: Located near the monument, this café offers snacks, sandwiches, and salads, with a focus on local ingredients.
2. **The State Game Lodge's Custer State Park Restaurant**: Located in Custer State Park, this restaurant serves American cuisine, including buffalo burgers and bison meatloaf.

These are just a few of the many options in the area. You can also explore the local farmers' markets or roadside stands for fresh produce and snacks. Enjoy your dining experiences during your trip to Mount Rushmore!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1
Today's date: <date>
</env>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6c49646a

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains extractable durable personal travel history and trip-planning preferences plus broader reusable context from recommendations and decisions, and force_memory_write requires a non-noop route.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/05/20 (Sat) 05:58
Transcript:
user: I'm planning another road trip and I'd like to know the best route from Denver to Mount Rushmore. By the way, speaking of road trips, I just got back from an amazing 4-day trip to Yellowstone National Park with my family last month, where we covered a total of 1,200 miles.
assistant: That sounds like an amazing trip to Yellowstone! 1,200 miles in 4 days is quite an adventure! I'm sure you must have seen some incredible sights.

Now, let's plan your next road trip to Mount Rushmore! The most popular and scenic route from Denver to Mount Rushmore is approximately 570 miles and takes around 8-9 hours of driving time, depending on traffic and the number of stops you make along the way. Here's a suggested route:

**Day 1: Denver, CO to Mount Rushmore, SD**

1. From Denver, take I-25 N towards Cheyenne, WY (approximately 100 miles, 1.5 hours)
2. Continue on I-25 N to I-90 E towards Rapid City, SD (approximately 270 miles, 4 hours)
3. Take exit 57 for US-16 E towards Mount Rushmore National Memorial (approximately 25 miles, 30 minutes)

**Scenic Stops along the way:**

* Cheyenne, WY: Stop by the Wyoming State Capitol building, the Cheyenne Frontier Days Old West Museum, or the Terry Bison Ranch.
* Wall, SD: Visit the famous Wall Drug Store, a popular roadside attraction.
* Badlands National Park, SD: Take a short detour to explore the stunning Badlands Loop Scenic Byway (SD-240) and enjoy the unique geological formations.

**Tips and Recommendations:**

* Be sure to check the Mount Rushmore National Memorial website for any road closures or construction before your trip.
* Consider visiting the Crazy Horse Memorial, a massive mountain sculpture located just 17 miles from Mount Rushmore.
* Book your accommodations in Rapid City or Keystone, SD, as they offer a wide range of hotels, restaurants, and amenities.
* Don't forget to try some local cuisine, like chislic (a South Dakota specialty) or a classic American burger at a roadside diner.

Enjoy your road trip to Mount Rushmore!
user: That sounds like a great route. Can you recommend any good camping sites near Mount Rushmore? We're considering camping instead of staying in a hotel.
assistant: Camping near Mount Rushmore is a fantastic way to experience the great outdoors and be close to the monument. Here are some excellent camping options to consider:

**National Park Service Campgrounds:**

1. **Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA**: This Kampgrounds of America (KOA) location is within walking distance to the monument and offers tent sites, RV sites, and cabins. Amenities include a pool, playground, and convenience store.
2. **Custer State Park**: Located about 20 miles from Mount Rushmore, this state park offers several campgrounds with varying amenities. The Sylvan Lake Campground is a popular option, with electric hookups and modern restrooms.

**Private Campgrounds:**

1. **Rushmore RV Resort**: This resort offers full-hookup RV sites, tent sites, and cabins. Amenities include a pool, hot tub, and convenience store.
2. **American Presidents Resort**: This resort features RV sites, tent sites, and cabins, as well as an outdoor pool, playground, and BBQ area.
3. **Keystone RV Park**: Located in the heart of Keystone, this park offers full-hookup RV sites, tent sites, and cabins. Amenities include a pool, hot tub, and convenience store.

**National Forest Campgrounds:**

1. **Deer Mountain Campground**: Located in the Black Hills National Forest, about 10 miles from Mount Rushmore, this campground offers dispersed camping with basic amenities.
2. **Horsethief Lake Campground**: Another option in the Black Hills National Forest, about 15 miles from Mount Rushmore, with basic amenities and scenic views.

**Tips and Reservations:**

* Make reservations well in advance, especially during peak season (June to August).
* Check the campground's website or contact them directly for availability and to book your spot.
* Be prepared for variable weather conditions, including thunderstorms and cooler evenings, even during the summer.
* Bring insect repellent, as mosquitoes and other bugs can be prevalent in the Black Hills.

Remember to follow the rules and regulations of the campground you choose, and enjoy your camping experience near Mount Rushmore!
user: I'm interested in the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA. Can you tell me more about the amenities and activities they offer? Also, do they have any RV rentals available?
assistant: The Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA is a fantastic choice for camping near the monument. Here's what you can expect:

**Amenities:**

1. **Camp Sites:** The KOA offers a range of camp sites, including:
	* Tent sites with electric hookups
	* RV sites with full hookups (water, electric, and sewer)
	* Deluxe RV sites with patios and grills
	* Cabin rentals (see below)
2. **Amenities:**
	* **Pool:** A large outdoor pool with a water slide and separate kiddie pool
	* **Playground:** A playground area for kids with climbing structures and swings
	* **Convenience Store:** A well-stocked store with groceries, souvenirs, and RV supplies
	* **Laundry:** Coin-operated laundry facilities
	* **Restrooms:** Clean, modern restrooms with showers and flush toilets
	* **Wi-Fi:** Free Wi-Fi throughout the campground
	* **Dog Park:** A fenced dog park for your furry friends to exercise
3. **Recreational Activities:**
	* **Mini Golf:** A 18-hole mini golf course on site
	* **Game Room:** An indoor game room with arcade games and pool tables
	* **Hiking Trails:** Access to nearby hiking trails, including the Presidential Trail that takes you to the base of Mount Rushmore
	* **Shuttle Service:** A shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial (seasonal)

**Cabin Rentals:**

The KOA offers a range of cabin rentals, including:

1. **Deluxe Cabins:** These cabins feature a full kitchen, private bathroom, and sleeping accommodations for up to 6 people.
2. **Kamping Kabin:** A more rustic option with a kitchenette and sleeping accommodations for up to 4 people.
3. **Tent Cabin:** A tent-style cabin with sleeping accommodations for up to 4 people.

**RV Rentals:**

Yes, the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA offers RV rentals! They have a fleet of RVs available for rent, ranging from small travel trailers to larger motorhomes. The RV rentals come with amenities like kitchenettes, bathrooms, and sleeping accommodations.

**RV Rental Options:**

1. **Travel Trailers:** Sleep 2-4 people, with kitchenette and bathroom
2. **Motorhomes:** Sleep 4-6 people, with full kitchen and bathroom
3. **Pop-Up Campers:** Sleep 2-4 people, with kitchenette and bathroom

**Reservations:**

To book a campsite, cabin, or RV rental, visit the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA website or call them directly at (800) 562-1885. Be sure to make reservations well in advance, especially during peak season.

Remember to check the KOA's website for any updates on amenities, activities, and RV rentals, as they may change seasonally.
user: I think we'll go with the deluxe cabin rental. Can you tell me more about the shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial? Is it free, and how often does it run?
assistant: The deluxe cabin rental is a great choice.

**Shuttle Service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial:**

The Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA offers a convenient shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial, making it easy to visit the monument without worrying about parking or traffic. Here are the details:

**Shuttle Service Details:**

1. **Seasonal Operation:** The shuttle service typically runs from late May to early October, coinciding with the peak tourist season.
2. **Frequency:** The shuttle runs every 30 minutes, starting from 8:00 AM to 6:00 PM. The last shuttle departs the KOA at 5:30 PM.
3. **Duration:** The shuttle ride takes approximately 10-15 minutes each way.
4. **Pickup/Drop-off:** The shuttle picks up and drops off at the KOA's main office. From there, you'll be taken to the Mount Rushmore National Memorial parking lot.
5. **Cost:** The shuttle service is **free** for KOA guests! You don't need to pay anything extra to use this convenient service.

**Tips:**

1. **Plan Ahead:** Be sure to check the shuttle schedule in advance to plan your day accordingly.
2. **Arrive Early:** To avoid long lines, consider taking an early shuttle to Mount Rushmore National Memorial.
3. **Bring Water and Snacks:** Bring water, snacks, and sunscreen to ensure a comfortable trip.
4. **Be Prepared for Security:** Be prepared for security checks at the memorial, and plan accordingly.

By using the shuttle service, you'll save time, avoid parking hassles, and enjoy a convenient trip to Mount Rushmore National Memorial. Just remember to check the KOA's website or contact them directly for any updates on the shuttle service schedule.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any more questions.
user: I'm glad I could get some helpful information about the shuttle service. By the way, I was thinking about our Yellowstone trip last month, and I realized that we drove around 300 miles on the first day to reach Jackson, Wyoming. That was a long drive, but the scenic views were totally worth it.
assistant: I'm glad you have fond memories of your Yellowstone trip! 300 miles on the first day is indeed a long drive, but as you said, the scenic views must have been breathtaking. Jackson, Wyoming, is a beautiful town, and I'm sure it was a great starting point for your Yellowstone adventure.

The drive from Denver to Jackson is quite scenic, with the Rocky Mountains as a stunning backdrop. You must have passed through some picturesque towns and landscapes, like the Snowy Range and the Wind River Canyon. The Grand Teton National Park, just north of Jackson, is also a treat, with its towering peaks and pristine lakes.

It's great that you prioritized enjoying the views over the long drive. Sometimes, the journey is just as important as the destination, and it sounds like you made the most of your time on the road.

Now, I'm excited for your upcoming trip to Mount Rushmore! With the KOA's shuttle service and the deluxe cabin rental, you'll be all set for a comfortable and convenient trip. Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further recommendations.
user: I'm thinking about the food options near Mount Rushmore. Are there any good restaurants or cafes in the area that you would recommend?
assistant: Mount Rushmore is surrounded by the beautiful Black Hills, and you'll find a variety of delicious food options in the nearby towns of Keystone, Hill City, and Rapid City. Here are some recommendations:

**Keystone:**

1. **The Keystone Café**: A local favorite serving breakfast, lunch, and dinner. Try their famous buffalo burgers or homemade pie.
2. **The Ruby House Restaurant**: Offers upscale American cuisine with a focus on local ingredients. Enjoy their patio seating with mountain views.
3. **The Alpine Inn**: This German-inspired restaurant serves traditional dishes like schnitzel and sauerbraten, along with American favorites.

**Hill City:**

1. **The Hill City Café**: A cozy café serving breakfast, lunch, and baked goods. Try their cinnamon rolls or homemade soup.
2. **The Alpine Slide Restaurant**: Located near the Hill City Alpine Slide, this restaurant serves burgers, sandwiches, and salads, with a focus on locally sourced ingredients.
3. **The 1880 Train's Hill City Depot Café**: This café is located in the historic train depot and offers sandwiches, salads, and snacks, along with a souvenir shop.

**Rapid City:**

1. **The Independent Ale House**: A craft brewery and restaurant serving burgers, sandwiches, and salads, with a focus on local ingredients.
2. **The Firehouse Brewing Company**: Another local brewery with a restaurant serving American cuisine, including burgers, steaks, and pasta dishes.
3. **Delmonico Grill**: An upscale restaurant serving steak, seafood, and pasta dishes in a historic building.

**Other Recommendations:**

1. **Mount Rushmore's Carver's Café**: Located near the monument, this café offers snacks, sandwiches, and salads, with a focus on local ingredients.
2. **The State Game Lodge's Custer State Park Restaurant**: Located in Custer State Park, this restaurant serves American cuisine, including buffalo burgers and bison meatloaf.

These are just a few of the many options in the area. You can also explore the local farmers' markets or roadside stands for fresh produce and snacks. Enjoy your dining experiences during your trip to Mount Rushmore!

Can you tell me more about the shuttle service to Mount Rushmore National Memorial?","evidence_reason":"The user explicitly chose the deluxe cabin rental and immediately connected that choice to learning about the shuttle service for visiting the memorial.","importance":4,"keywords":["Mount Rushmore","KOA","deluxe cabin","shuttle service","camping instead of hotel","road trip"],"note_type":"plan","rationale":"This captures a trip-planning direction involving multiple related choices and logistical rationale, not just a single atomic fact.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's Mount Rushmore road trip planning context"},"summary":"For the planned Denver-to-Mount Rushmore trip, the user moved from considering camping instead of a hotel to selecting the Mount Rushmore National Memorial KOA deluxe cabin rental, with the KOA shuttle service as part of the convenience plan for visiting Mount Rushmore National Memorial.","tags":["travel_plan","mount_rushmore","camping","koa"],"title":"Mount Rushmore trip direction: camp at KOA in deluxe cabin and use shuttle","valid_from":"2023-05-20T05:58:00Z","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["User took a 4-day family trip to Yellowstone National Park in April 2023.","The Yellowstone trip covered about 1,200 miles total.","The first day of the Yellowstone trip involved driving about 300 miles to Jackson, Wyoming.","User felt the scenic views on the long first-day drive were worth it."],"confidence":0.95,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"primary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<5>","role":"secondary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I realized that we drove around 300 miles on the first day to reach Jackson, Wyoming. That was a long drive, but the scenic views were totally worth it.","evidence_reason":"The quoted user statement gives the first-day mileage, destination, and positive rationale for the long scenic drive; the earlier same transcript gives the 4-day and 1,200-mile Yellowstone context.","importance":3,"keywords":["Yellowstone","family road trip","4-day trip","1200 miles","300 miles","Jackson Wyoming","scenic views"],"note_type":"context","rationale":"This is reusable travel-history context with subjective rationale about scenic drives, combining related details from the session rather than a single isolated claim.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's past Yellowstone family road trip experience"},"summary":"The user's recent Yellowstone National Park family road trip was a 4-day, approximately 1,200-mile trip; on the first day they drove around 300 miles to reach Jackson, Wyoming, and considered the scenic views worth the long drive.","tags":["travel_history","road_trip","yellowstone"],"title":"Yellowstone trip context: long scenic family road trip","valid_from":"2023-05-20T05:58:00Z","valid_to":null}]}
